trail_markers.text: Michelle described five key industries that Amplitude focuses its efforts on. Which of the following is one of those five?
options: Travel and hospitality
Media and entertainment
Government and public sector
Telecommunications

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: Michelle listed the five key industries as retail and e-commerce, B2B SaaS (technology), financial services, media and entertainment, and healthcare. Travel, government, and telecom were mentioned by attendees as industries they had previously sold into — but are not among Amplitude's five focus industries.

incorrect_feedback.label: Fire Starter! Incorrect:
incorrect_feedback.explanation: Michelle listed the five key industries as retail and e-commerce, B2B SaaS (technology), financial services, media and entertainment, and healthcare. clips.title: Day 2: Top of Funnel (TOFU) – MQLs & Inbounds

trail_markers.text: According to the session, what was the primary goal of the TOFU project — and what specific improvement did it deliver in lead delivery speed?
options: To reduce duplicate MQLs — leads now arrive deduplicated within 24 hours instead of 48
To get leads to reps faster so they can work them faster — leads now arrive in under 5 minutes instead of the previous 30 minutes
To improve lead quality scoring — person scores now update in real time instead of overnight
To simplify Salesforce — all lead management was moved out of the GTM dashboard into a single contact list

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: The presenter explained that the goal of the TOFU project was to get leads to reps faster so they could work them faster. The marketing team deprecated several platforms and untangled the enrichment process so leads now arrive in under 5 minutes — down from the previous 30-minute window.

incorrect_feedback.label: Fire Starter! Incorrect:
incorrect_feedback.explanation: The presenter explained that the goal of the TOFU project was to get leads to reps faster so they could work them faster. The marketing team deprecated several platforms and untangled the enrichment process so leads now arrive in under 5 minutes — down from the previous 30-minute window.

trail_markers.text: The session introduced a new sales stage called the SAL. What does SAL stand for, and where does it sit in the sales process?
options: Sales Aligned Lead — it sits between S1 and S2, triggered when a meeting has occurred
Sales Accepted Lead — it sits between MQL and S0, triggered when positive activity has been taken toward booking a meeting
Sales Actioned Lead — it sits before the MQL stage, triggered when a rep first views a lead
Sales Assigned Lead — it sits between S0 and S1, triggered when a meeting is booked and confirmed

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: SAL stands for Sales Accepted Lead. The presenter explained it sits right between MQL and S0. An SAL means the rep has taken positive activity — a phone call or email — and is working toward booking a meeting. S0 is when the meeting is booked, and S1 is when the meeting has happened.

incorrect_feedback.label: Fire Starter! Incorrect:
incorrect_feedback.explanation: SAL stands for Sales Accepted Lead. The presenter explained it sits right between MQL and S0. An SAL means the rep has taken positive activity — a phone call or email — and is working toward booking a meeting. S0 is when the meeting is booked, and S1 is when the meeting has happened.

trail_markers.text: The session explained that MQL SLA hours are based on working hours. A hand raiser MQL arrives at 7pm on a Friday. When does the SLA clock start?
options: Immediately — the SLA starts the moment the MQL is assigned regardless of day or time
Saturday at 9am — the next calendar day when work could reasonably begin
Monday at 9am — when working hours resume in the rep's time zone
Friday at 9pm — two hours after the MQL was received, since hand raiser SLAs are one hour

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: The presenter was explicit: SLA hours are working hours based on the rep's time zone and region. Using this exact example, he said if a hand raiser MQL arrives at 7pm on a Friday, the rep is not on the hook to resolve it in one hour at 7pm — the clock starts Monday at 9am when working hours resume.

incorrect_feedback.label: Fire Starter! Incorrect:
incorrect_feedback.explanation: The presenter was explicit: SLA hours are working hours based on the rep's time zone and region. Using this exact example, he said if a hand raiser MQL arrives at 7pm on a Friday, the rep is not on the hook to resolve it in one hour at 7pm — the clock starts Monday at 9am when working hours resume.

trail_markers.text: If an MQL goes into breach, what is the escalation sequence that follows?
options: The MQL is automatically disqualified after 24 hours and reassigned to another rep on the team
The breach is logged silently — the rep and manager are notified by email at the end of the week
The rep is tagged in the breach channel along with their FLM, who has 24 hours to follow up. If the FLM doesn't resolve it, the VP is tagged and has 48 hours to work with the manager to resolve it
The VP is notified immediately and has 24 hours to reassign the MQL before it is escalated to the CRO

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: The presenter walked through the breach escalation sequence: the rep and their frontline manager (FLM) are tagged in the breach channel. The FLM has 24 hours to follow up with the rep directly — they cannot reply in the channel. If the FLM doesn't resolve it, the VP is tagged and has 48 hours to work through the manager to get it resolved.

incorrect_feedback.label: Fire Starter! Incorrect:
incorrect_feedback.explanation: The presenter walked through the breach escalation sequence: the rep and their frontline manager (FLM) are tagged in the breach channel. The FLM has 24 hours to follow up with the rep directly — they cannot reply in the channel. If the FLM doesn't resolve it, the VP is tagged and has 48 hours to work through the manager to get it resolved.

trail_markers.text: Justin described the Sales Performance Dashboard built in Tableau. What is the SLA adherence target the team is aiming for right off the bat?
options: 100% — the expectation is full adherence with exceptions handled case by case
90% — a high bar that accounts for occasional back-to-back meeting conflicts
80% — a realistic target that accounts for the varying demands across different roles
70% — a baseline for the first quarter that will increase as reps build the habit

correct_feedback.explanation: Justin stated explicitly that 80% SLA adherence is the target right off the bat. He noted the presenter had mentioned the same — they are not expecting 100% and recognize that different teams will perform at different levels, with SDRs likely higher and AEs potentially lower due to more customer-facing meeting demands.

incorrect_feedback.label: Fire Starter! Incorrect:
incorrect_feedback.explanation: Justin stated explicitly that 80% SLA adherence is the target right off the bat. He noted the presenter had mentioned the same — they are not expecting 100% and recognize that different teams will perform at different levels, with SDRs likely higher and AEs potentially lower due to more customer-facing meeting demands.

clips.title: Day 3: GTM Launch Pad (SFDC Sales Dashboard)

trail_markers.text: Matt described the GTM Launch Pad as being designed for everyone in go-to-market. How far up and down the org is it used?
options: It is designed exclusively for AEs and their FLMs — leadership uses a separate executive dashboard
It is designed for everyone from the CRO all the way down to AEs and SDRs — everyone in go-to-market uses the same platform
It is designed for AEs, SDRs, and velocity reps — VPs and above have their own dedicated analytics environment
It is designed for individual contributors only — managers have a separate version with additional controls and permissions

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: Matt explicitly stated the dashboard is designed so everyone in go-to-market can use it — all the way up from Nate, the CRO, all the way down to AEs and their SDR counterparts. It is designed to bring transparency and visibility across all go-to-market teams so everyone is looking at the same data.

incorrect_feedback.label: Fire Starter! Incorrect:

It is designed to bring transparency and visibility across all go-to-market teams so everyone is looking at the same data.

trail_markers.text: Matt explained that when reviewing renewals, AEs should keep a rolling view of upcoming renewals. What time window did he recommend?
options: A rolling 3-month view — close enough to act on without being overwhelming
A rolling 6-month view — enough runway to drive health, flag churn risks, and manage renewals proactively
A rolling 12-month view — the full annual cycle gives the most complete picture of your renewal base
Current quarter only — focusing beyond the current quarter creates noise and distracts from what needs to close now

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: Matt was clear: when thinking through renewals and planning, the team wants AEs to keep a rolling 6-month view. This gives enough runway to drive customer health, flag churn risks where they exist, and manage renewals before they become late-stage problems.

incorrect_feedback.label: Fire Starter! Incorrect:

trail_markers.text: Matt described the aspirational pipeline coverage ratio for next quarter. What is it — and is there a difference between enterprise and emerging enterprise reps?
options: 5x for enterprise and 4x for emerging enterprise — higher multiples reflect longer sales cycles
3x for both enterprise and emerging enterprise — a single standard applies across all AE segments
3x for enterprise and anything over 2x for emerging enterprise — EE reps close more deals in-quarter so they can succeed with lower coverage
4x for enterprise and 3x for emerging enterprise — the higher multiple reflects enterprise deal complexity

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: Matt explained that 3x next quarter pipeline coverage is the general benchmark — very good for enterprise. For emerging enterprise, he noted those reps typically have more deals that are qualified and closed in-quarter, so 2x coverage is realistically where they can be very successful. Anything below 2x would start to generate concern from managers.

incorrect_feedback.label: Fire Starter! Incorrect:
incorrect_feedback.explanation: Matt explained that 3x next quarter pipeline coverage is the general benchmark — very good for enterprise. For emerging enterprise, he noted those reps typically have more deals that are qualified and closed in-quarter, so 2x coverage is realistically where they can be very successful. Anything below 2x would start to generate concern from managers.

trail_markers.text: Matt introduced the Prospect Score in the Target Accounts section. What threshold did he say Amplitude considers to be a high or very high prospect score?
options: Any score above 10 is considered high — the majority of closed-won business comes from accounts at this level
Any score above 15 is considered high or very high — and about 90 to 95 percent of closed-won business comes from accounts above a medium prospect score
Any score above 20 is considered high — scores between 10 and 20 are medium and still worth pursuing
Any score above 25 is considered very high — scores between 15 and 25 are high and represent your primary focus

correct_feedback.explanation: Matt stated that generally anything over a score of 15 is considered to be a high or very high prospect score. He also shared that about 90 to 95 percent of Amplitude's closed-won business comes from accounts that have above a medium prospect score — making this score a strong and confident indicator of where reps should focus their efforts.

trail_markers.text: Matt described what a CSQL is and why it matters. What is a CSQL and how is it created?
options: A Customer Success Qualified Lead — created by the marketing team when a customer engages with an upsell campaign
A Customer Sales Qualified Lead — created automatically by the system when a customer's usage exceeds a set threshold
A Customer Success Qualified Lead — created by the CSM when a customer expresses interest in a product during a customer conversation, signaling a warm expansion opportunity for the AE
A Customer Signals Qualified Lead — generated by 6sense when an existing customer shows high intent behavior on the Amplitude website

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: Matt explained that a CSQL is essentially the CSM creating a qualified lead for the AE. When the CSM meets with a customer and the customer expresses interest in a product — like Experimentation — the CSM creates a CSQL. Matt called these 'the hottest of hot leads' because the CSM is directly telling you the customer mentioned it. He said if you are following up on them properly, the open CSQLs section should show no results.

incorrect_feedback.label: Fire Starter! Incorrect:
incorrect_feedback.explanation: Matt explained that a CSQL is essentially the CSM creating a qualified lead for the AE. When the CSM meets with a customer and the customer expresses interest in a product — like Experimentation — the CSM creates a CSQL. Matt called these 'the hottest of hot leads' because the CSM is directly telling you the customer mentioned it. He said if you are following up on them properly, the open CSQLs section should show no results.

trail_markers.text: ZoomInfo has a monthly credit limit of 1,000 per user. What exactly counts as one credit — and what happens if you use all 1,000 before the month ends?
options: One credit = one search query. If you run out, you can request more from your manager at any time.
One credit = one contact add. If you use all 1,000, they do not replenish until the first of the following month — no exceptions, no way to get more early.
One credit = one contact export to Salesforce. Unused credits from teammates can be transferred to you if needed.
One credit = one contact enrichment. Credits roll over if you have any remaining from the previous month.

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: JT was explicit: one credit equals one contact add. Credits replenish on the first of the following month — not before. He said he cannot give more credits, cannot regenerate them, and cannot magically gift them. Credits also do not roll over — it is 1,000 use it or lose it, reset on the first of every month.

incorrect_feedback.label: Fire Starter! Incorrect:
incorrect_feedback.explanation: JT was explicit: one credit equals one contact add. Credits replenish on the first of the following month — not before. He said he cannot give more credits, cannot regenerate them, and cannot magically gift them. Credits also do not roll over — it is 1,000 use it or lose it, reset on the first of every month.

trail_markers.text: In ZoomInfo, what does a Salesforce icon next to a contact's name indicate — and what is the credit implication?
options: It means the contact is a high-priority lead flagged by marketing — adding them costs 2 credits instead of 1
It means the contact already exists in Salesforce. The first export or enrichment costs 1 credit, but after that they can be re-enriched unlimited times within the calendar year for no credit
It means the contact has already been sequenced in Outreach — adding them will create a duplicate and should be avoided
It means the contact has opted out of ZoomInfo sharing and cannot be added to Salesforce through ZoomInfo

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: JT explained that a Salesforce icon means the contact already exists in Salesforce — they are being enriched, not net-new added. The first time anyone enriches that contact costs 1 credit. After that first enrichment, the contact can be re-enriched as many times as needed within the calendar year at no additional credit. If there is no Salesforce icon, the contact is net new and the rep who adds them owns that contact.

incorrect_feedback.label: Fire Starter! Incorrect:
incorrect_feedback.explanation: JT explained that a Salesforce icon means the contact already exists in Salesforce — they are being enriched, not net-new added. The first time anyone enriches that contact costs 1 credit. After that first enrichment, the contact can be re-enriched as many times as needed within the calendar year at no additional credit. If there is no Salesforce icon, the contact is net new and the rep who adds them owns that contact.

trail_markers.text: JT explained the importance of tagging contacts in ZoomInfo. What problem does the tagging system solve — and where do tags follow the contact?
options: Tags categorize contacts by ICP fit score so reps can prioritize outreach — they exist only within ZoomInfo and reset when exported
Tags identify which buying group a contact belongs to and sync automatically to Salesforce fields without additional setup

Tags are your name or a custom label. Because reps don't own all contacts in Salesforce, tagging tracks which contacts you added — and tags follow from ZoomInfo to Salesforce to Outreach throughout your workflow
Tags mark contacts who are already in an active Outreach sequence so you don't accidentally re-add someone

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: JT explained that because contact ownership in Salesforce only applies to net-new contacts, tags are how you track all contacts you've added — including enrichments you don't technically own. You create a tag with your name, apply it when exporting, and it follows the contact from ZoomInfo to Salesforce to Outreach. He called it one of the two most important takeaways from the session alongside the 1,000 credit limit.

incorrect_feedback.label: Fire Starter! Incorrect:
incorrect_feedback.explanation: JT explained that because contact ownership in Salesforce only applies to net-new contacts, tags are how you track all contacts you've added — including enrichments you don't technically own. You create a tag with your name, apply it when exporting, and it follows the contact from ZoomInfo to Salesforce to Outreach. He called it one of the two most important takeaways from the session alongside the 1,000 credit limit.

trail_markers.text: JT demonstrated how to narrow a ZoomInfo contact list using filters. Which filter reduced the list from 1.3 million contacts down to 290,000?
options: Filtering by industry — removing companies outside Amplitude's five target industries
Filtering by buying group and title level — limiting to product decision makers at VP level
Filtering by contact info requiring both a business email AND a mobile number — making both mandatory
Filtering by confidence score at 95 or above — keeping only contacts with the highest accuracy rating

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: JT demonstrated step by step: starting from 1.3 million contacts, he required both business email AND mobile number using the AND toggle — making both mandatory. That single filter reduced the list from 1.3 million to 290,000. He then showed that a 90th percentile confidence score filter reduced it further to 232,000, and adding the product buying group with VP title level brought it down to a targeted 544 contacts.

incorrect_feedback.label: Fire Starter! Incorrect:
incorrect_feedback.explanation: JT demonstrated step by step: starting from 1.3 million contacts, he required both business email AND mobile number using the AND toggle — making both mandatory. That single filter reduced the list from 1.3 million to 290,000. He then showed that a 90th percentile confidence score filter reduced it further to 232,000, and adding the product buying group with VP title level brought it down to a targeted 544 contacts.

trail_markers.text: JT explained what happens when a contact has opted out of having their information shared on ZoomInfo. What does this actually mean for how you can still reach them?
options: A ZoomInfo opt-out means they have requested no commercial contact from any platform — they should be removed from all outreach
A ZoomInfo opt-out only means they don't want their information shared publicly on ZoomInfo — you can still reach them via LinkedIn InMail, Sales Navigator, or by manually adding them to Salesforce
A ZoomInfo opt-out flags them as do-not-contact in Salesforce as well, preventing them from being added to any Outreach sequences
A ZoomInfo opt-out means their contact data won't populate in the Chrome extension but they can still be added manually through the ZoomInfo dashboard

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: JT was clear: opting out of ZoomInfo only means the contact doesn't want their information shared publicly on ZoomInfo's platform. It does not prevent outreach through other channels. LinkedIn InMail, Sales Navigator, or manually guessing their email based on company conventions are all still valid approaches. JT even noted a savvy AE raised this exact point during the session.

incorrect_feedback.label: Fire Starter! Incorrect:
incorrect_feedback.explanation: JT was clear: opting out of ZoomInfo only means the contact doesn't want their information shared publicly on ZoomInfo's platform. It does not prevent outreach through other channels. LinkedIn InMail, Sales Navigator, or manually guessing their email based on company conventions are all still valid approaches. JT even noted a savvy AE raised this exact point during the session.

trail_markers.text: Francis described his three primary responsibilities on the competitive strategy team. Which of the following was one of them?
options: Building product roadmap features specifically designed to counter competitor capabilities
Conducting win/loss interviews with customers after deals to understand why Amplitude was chosen or not chosen
Managing pricing negotiations directly with enterprise prospects who are evaluating competitors
Running quarterly competitive benchmarking reports for the board of directors

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: Francis described three pillars of his work: keeping Amplitude's execs and leaders informed on what is happening in the market — including win rates against competitors like Mixpanel or Adobe; conducting win/loss interviews by paying customers to discuss their decision-making process; and sharing that intelligence with the field so reps know what to say in head-to-head situations or rip-and-replace scenarios.

incorrect_feedback.label: Fire Starter! Incorrect:
incorrect_feedback.explanation: Francis described three pillars of his work: keeping Amplitude's execs and leaders informed on what is happening in the market — including win rates against competitors like Mixpanel or Adobe; conducting win/loss interviews by paying customers to discuss their decision-making process; and sharing that intelligence with the field so reps know what to say in head-to-head situations or rip-and-replace scenarios.

trail_markers.text: Francis explained when a full rip-and-replace of Adobe is most likely to succeed versus when Amplitude and Adobe coexist. What did he say is the primary condition for a full rip-and-replace?
options: When the customer is primarily a B2B SaaS company with no marketing team using Adobe's campaign tools
When the customer wants to focus a significant amount of effort on a mobile app or product — because Adobe cannot compete as well there
When the customer has already been using Amplitude for analytics and wants to consolidate all blades under one vendor
When the customer is in the healthcare or financial services industry where Adobe faces regulatory limitations

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: Francis explained that Amplitude coexists alongside Adobe in many large enterprise accounts — Nike, Home Depot, Target — because those companies still depend on Adobe's marketing capabilities that Amplitude does not replicate. The opportunity for a full rip-and-replace is specifically when a company wants to invest heavily in a mobile app or product, because that is where Adobe cannot compete as well. The strategy he described is to get a foot in the door where Adobe is weakest, then grow from there.

incorrect_feedback.label: Fire Starter! Incorrect:
incorrect_feedback.explanation: Francis explained that Amplitude coexists alongside Adobe in many large enterprise accounts — Nike, Home Depot, Target — because those companies still depend on Adobe's marketing capabilities that Amplitude does not replicate. The opportunity for a full rip-and-replace is specifically when a company wants to invest heavily in a mobile app or product, because that is where Adobe cannot compete as well. The strategy he described is to get a foot in the door where Adobe is weakest, then grow from there.

trail_markers.text: Francis described Mixpanel's primary competitive play and the pricing situation reps should be prepared for. What is it?
options: Mixpanel leads with enterprise data governance features and positions Amplitude as too focused on SMB use cases

Mixpanel claims to be the best-in-breed analytics solution at the lowest price — and will aggressively price cut, especially in Asia where they will give the product away for free or next to free to win the logo
Mixpanel leads with session replay and experimentation features to compete across all of Amplitude's blades simultaneously
Mixpanel focuses on mid-market accounts where Amplitude's platform pricing creates a barrier, offering lower entry-level plans

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: Francis described Mixpanel's play clearly: they claim to be the best-in-breed analytics solution that gives the most features at the lowest price, and they try to turn Amplitude's multi-blade advantage against Amplitude by saying it has lost focus. They aggressively price cut — and in the Asia market specifically, they will give the product away for free or next to free to win a logo. He also noted Mixpanel doesn't scale for enterprise and lacks the data governance capabilities Amplitude provides.

incorrect_feedback.label: Fire Starter! Incorrect:
incorrect_feedback.explanation: Francis described Mixpanel's play clearly: they claim to be the best-in-breed analytics solution that gives the most features at the lowest price, and they try to turn Amplitude's multi-blade advantage against Amplitude by saying it has lost focus. They aggressively price cut — and in the Asia market specifically, they will give the product away for free or next to free to win a logo. He also noted Mixpanel doesn't scale for enterprise and lacks the data governance capabilities Amplitude provides.

trail_markers.text: Francis explained the main argument against the in-house build competitor. What is the core challenge reps should surface when a prospect says they built their own analytics solution?
options: In-house builds lack event tracking capabilities and cannot match Amplitude's data model for behavioral analytics
In-house builds create vendor lock-in because they are built on proprietary infrastructure that cannot integrate with modern data stacks

In-house builds appear cheaper but ignore the real cost of expensive engineers building and constantly maintaining the solution — and the opportunity cost of those engineers not doing other mission-critical work
In-house builds are typically built on legacy SQL infrastructure that cannot support real-time data and will need to be replaced within two years

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: Francis said the biggest competitor any company faces is doing nothing — staying with whatever hacky solution they put together. When a prospect says they built their own analytics solution, the argument is not that it doesn't work. It is: how much did it cost your expensive engineers to build it, how much is it costing them to maintain it, and what other mission-critical work could those engineers be doing instead of constantly repairing the homegrown tool?

incorrect_feedback.label: Fire Starter! Incorrect:
incorrect_feedback.explanation: Francis said the biggest competitor any company faces is doing nothing — staying with whatever hacky solution they put together. When a prospect says they built their own analytics solution, the argument is not that it doesn't work. It is: how much did it cost your expensive engineers to build it, how much is it costing them to maintain it, and what other mission-critical work could those engineers be doing instead of constantly repairing the homegrown tool?

trail_markers.text: During the Jeopardy exercise, Francis explained why data governance matters so much to customers. What was his way of making it real for prospects?
options: Bad data governance leads to GDPR violations and regulatory fines — especially in healthcare and finance where data privacy laws are strict

Bad data governance prevents marketing teams from running A/B tests because the event tracking is too inconsistent to produce statistically significant results

Payment delinquency — customers who are 30 or more days past due on an invoice are the strongest predictor of churn regardless of their product usage
Gainsight product health score — a red health score is the single most reliable indicator of churn and overrides all other signals including utilization and payment history

He recommended that if a customer is below 50%, the AE should be working with the CSM on how to get the customer to use more of what they are paying for. clips.title: Day 12: Customer Stories (Power to Your Stories)

trail_markers.text: JT described the four-part structure every great story follows. What are the four parts in order?
options: Hook, context, social proof, and a clear call to action that drives the prospect toward the next step

Introduction (hero's current situation), conflict (the crisis that compels action), turning point (where the hero acts to resolve the conflict), and the end (the outcome showing growth, impact, or success)
Problem statement, solution overview, quantified results, and a recommended next step for the prospect
Discovery phase, challenge acknowledgment, resolution through the product, and a closing recommendation

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: JT walked through the four-part story structure: the introduction sets the hero's current situation or context. The conflict is the crisis that compels the hero to act. The turning point is the pivotal moment where the hero actually takes action to resolve the conflict. And the end is the outcome that shows growth, impact, or success. He demonstrated each part through movie plots including Harry Potter and Titanic before applying the same framework to customer stories.

incorrect_feedback.label: Fire Starter! Incorrect:
incorrect_feedback.explanation: JT walked through the four-part story structure: the introduction sets the hero's current situation or context. trail_markers.text: Ari Hoffman explained what it means for a customer story to be 'personal.' What does personal mean in the context of customer storytelling?
options: Personal means sharing your own experiences as a seller — stories from your previous companies and deals are more relatable than third-party case studies

Personal means the story is about the people, not the technology — people are the heroes because prospects can empathize with other people, not with tools. Amplitude is the Millennium Falcon, not Han Solo.
Personal means the story is tailored to the specific industry of the prospect — vertical alignment makes a story feel personal and relevant
Personal means the story is shared one-on-one rather than in a group setting — intimacy of delivery is what makes a customer story feel personal

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: Ari explained that personal means the story is about the people, not the technology. People cannot relate to technology — they relate to other people. The customer is the hero because they are the ones who stuck their neck out to get budget, drove internal adoption, and whose reputation is on the line if the program fails. He used the Star Wars analogy: nobody leaves a movie saying 'I want to be the Millennium Falcon' — they say 'I want to be Han Solo.' But if you want to be Han Solo, you are flying the Millennium Falcon. Amplitude is the enabler, never the star.

incorrect_feedback.explanation: Ari explained that personal means the story is about the people, not the technology. People cannot relate to technology — they relate to other people. He used the Star Wars analogy: nobody leaves a movie saying 'I want to be the Millennium Falcon' — they say 'I want to be Han Solo.' trail_markers.text: Ari explained why a good story told at the wrong time can actually hurt rather than help. What is the key principle about relevance and timing?
options: Stories should only be told during formal demos — dropping a story in a discovery call before you understand the prospect's pain comes across as presumptuous

A good story told when the conversation has nothing to do with it can turn someone into a detractor — relevance requires actively listening first and only bringing in a story when it fits the moment, not waiting for your turn to tell it
Stories work best late in the sales cycle near the close — bringing them in too early dilutes their impact when the prospect needs to make a final decision
Timing is mostly about matching the story to the prospect's industry — vertical relevance is what makes a story feel right at any point in the conversation

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: Ari described watching a CSM on an EBR call who had a story prepared and waited for the right moment — but the conversation never connected to the story. They forced it in anyway, and it fell flat. He warned that a good story told at the wrong time can turn someone into a detractor rather than a promoter. The principle is to actively listen first, find the transitionary moment where the story naturally fits, and then bring it in. Not every fish eats the same bait — setting the hook requires timing, not just having a hook.

trail_markers.text: Julia Dillon described three benefits of telling customer stories. What are they?
options: Faster onboarding, higher product adoption, and better renewal rates
Building trust with real results instead of empty promises, demonstrating tangible business impact and ROI, and acting as a Swiss Army knife that works across prospecting, expansion, and retention
Differentiating from competitors, shortening the sales cycle, and reducing the need for expensive proof-of-concept trials
Generating referrals, improving win rates in competitive deals, and reducing customer acquisition cost

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:

Third, they are a Swiss Army knife — the same story can be adapted for a new prospect facing a similar problem, an expansion conversation with an existing customer, or even explaining what you do to someone outside work. She also cited the statistics: customer stories are 22 times more memorable, 68% of buyers say they influence business decisions, and 55% are more likely to buy after hearing one.

trail_markers.text: When configuring a 2026 PPL quote in CPQ, what is the first choice you must make when selecting a package — and what is the recommended usage term setting?

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:

After that, usage term should be set to annual whenever possible. The reasoning: if a customer has seasonality where they use more events or MTUs in certain months, an annual usage term gives them one large bucket to draw from across the year, which avoids overages. trail_markers.text: When entering event volume for a 2-year ramp deal, how do you enter the quantity in CPQ — and what does 'Save and Create Groups' do?
options: Enter total annual events in billions directly — Save and Create Groups locks both year prices simultaneously and prevents further edits

Enter the monthly equivalent in millions (divide annual events by 12) — Save and Create Groups creates the second year term automatically so you can configure different event volumes per year for a ramp deal
Enter total contract-period events divided by 24 months — Save and Create Groups generates a DocuSign envelope for both years at once
Enter the annual amount in thousands — Save and Create Groups merges both years into a single line item for simplified billing

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: CPQ takes event quantity as a monthly figure in millions. For 5 billion events annually: divide by 1,000 to convert to millions (5,000M), then divide by 12 to get the monthly equivalent (416.67). Enter 416.67 in the field. For a ramp deal with a 24-month term, clicking Save and Create Groups automatically creates the second year term (Term 2), where you can enter different event volumes — in the demo, 7 billion events in year 2 becomes 583.33 (7,000 ÷ 12). Always hit Quicksave after any quantity or pricing changes.

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: The presenter recommended Target Net Total over percentage discounts when you need an exact number. Because PSP and professional services are always standalone and excluded from the target net total calculation, you must subtract their costs manually before entering the number. Example: if the customer can pay $700K in year 2 and PSP costs $41,250, enter $658,750 as the target net total ($700K minus $41,250). CPQ then calculates the discount needed to hit that net. The same math applies if you also want to exclude implementation from year 1.

Once you know what approvals are firing, fill in the Notes field with deal context that addresses each approval point: why you're doing the deal, what discount was applied and why, any urgency like a signature target date. This gives approvers the information they need to move quickly rather than chasing the AE for context.
